title: Secrets Management for Modern Security

modules.title: Module 1: Why Leaked Secrets Are the Leading Cause of AI-Era Breaches
modules.sub: Understanding how exposed secrets fuel modern attacks
modules.description: Learners examine real-world breach case studies where leaked credentials and API keys served as initial attack vectors, including incidents accelerated by AI-generated code. They build a threat model that maps the most common secret exposure paths across development, deployment, and collaboration workflows.

modules.title: Module 2: Password Managers at Scale — Enterprise Deployment and Policy
modules.sub: Rolling out and governing password managers across large organizations
modules.description: Learners evaluate enterprise password manager platforms against organizational requirements including SSO integration, role-based access, and audit logging. They design a deployment and onboarding policy that enforces strong credential hygiene while minimizing friction for engineering and non-technical staff alike.

modules.title: Module 3: API Key Lifecycle Management — Generation, Rotation, and Revocation
modules.sub: Controlling API keys from creation through secure decommissioning
modules.description: Learners map the full lifecycle of an API key and identify the risk windows at each stage, from permissive initial scoping to forgotten long-lived tokens. They build a lifecycle policy that enforces least-privilege issuance, automated rotation schedules, and rapid revocation procedures triggered by detected exposure.

modules.title: Module 4: Secrets in Code — Scanning Repositories and Fixing Exposure Fast
modules.sub: Detecting and remediating secrets committed to source repositories
modules.description: Learners configure and run automated secret-scanning tools against sample repositories, interpreting results to prioritize which exposed secrets require immediate rotation. They practice an incident response workflow for repository exposure that covers revocation, history scrubbing, and post-mortem documentation.

modules.title: Module 5: Vault Tools and Secrets Orchestration for DevSecOps Pipelines
modules.sub: Injecting secrets securely into CI/CD pipelines using vault solutions
modules.description: Learners compare leading vault platforms and integrate a vault-based secret injection pattern into a representative CI/CD pipeline, replacing hardcoded credentials with dynamic, short-lived secrets. They configure access policies, audit logging, and break-glass procedures that balance security with pipeline reliability.

modules.title: Module 6: AI Coding Assistants and the New Risk of Accidental Secret Leakage
modules.sub: Addressing secret exposure risks introduced by AI-assisted development
modules.description: Learners analyze the specific ways AI coding assistants can surface, suggest, or inadvertently propagate secrets through prompt context, training data echoes, and auto-completed code patterns. They develop guardrails including pre-commit hooks, IDE-level scanning, and developer guidance tailored to AI-assisted workflows.

modules.title: Module 7: Building a Secrets Hygiene Culture Across Engineering Teams
modules.sub: Embedding lasting secrets management practices throughout the engineering organization
modules.description: Learners design a secrets hygiene program that combines policy, tooling enforcement, and developer education to shift secrets management left in the development lifecycle. They create measurable standards and feedback mechanisms — including audit dashboards and blameless incident reviews — that sustain cultural change over time.

synopsis: Hardcoded API keys, reused passwords, and secrets accidentally committed to public repositories remain the fastest path for attackers into production systems — and AI coding assistants have introduced new vectors for accidental leakage that most teams have not yet addressed. This course gives security engineers and DevSecOps professionals a rigorous, practical framework for managing secrets across the full development lifecycle.
rationale: The explosion of AI-generated code and LLM-assisted development has created a new class of secret-exposure risk that existing secrets management curricula and tooling guidance do not yet cover.
learning_outcome: Learners can audit their organization's secrets management posture, implement automated scanning and vault-based secret injection in CI/CD pipelines, and establish policies that account for AI-assisted coding workflows.
source_signals: Is it secure to store passwords as environment variables (rather than as plain text) in config files?
